title: Enable CPython unittest regression tests
description:
The single conformance umbrella: make M28 pass CPython-derived unittest regression tests.

Status (2026-07-01): 526/943 tests passing (conformance.json, scored by the python-unit-test loop). unittest itself currently runs on a custom minimal ./unittest.py stub - replacing it with CPython's real unittest package is part of this epic's endgame.

Structure:
- Per-test-file work items live under M28-jmst (M28-jmst.NN children, one per CPython test file).
- Root-cause fixes that unblock whole test families are tracked in the runtime-consolidation epic M28-j9o (format engine -> test_format, dict hash/eq keying -> test_hash, C3 MRO -> diamond inheritance) and the perf epic M28-e42e (native re path -> test_re and regex-bound timeouts).
- Absorbed the former duplicate umbrellas M28-nqhx (CPython 3.12 Test Suite Compatibility) and M28-7d53 (Python Compatibility Improvements).

Policy (CLAUDE.md): pure-Python stdlib runs directly; only C extensions get Go stubs; fix language features rather than stubbing pure-Python modules.

title: perf: model try/except in the resolver (functions with try no longer bail)
description: try is not in slotSafeForms, so any function containing try/except/finally falls off the slot path entirely -- same containment problem comprehensions and methods had. except-as binds a name (scoped to the handler, deleted after in CPython); finally/else are plain blocks. Plan: model try bodies as value positions; except-as target either gets a slot (with the CPython post-handler unbind semantics -- UnboundLocal after) or conservatively bails only when 'as' is present (still unlocks bare try/finally and typed except without as, the majority). exceptions case is 5.2x; also unlocks big stdlib coverage since try is pervasive. Gate: test_exceptions/test_with/test_contextlib name-set diffs.
close_reason: Landed: modern clause shapes modeled, as/except*/legacy bail. Quiet-try -16%, regex_match -14%, exceptions flat (module-level + raise-machinery-bound, ~21us/raise). All gates green, conformance identical. Follow-ups filed: tryNode pre-parsed clauses; raise-path cost.

title: perf: dotNode IR -- compile attribute access and method calls
description: attr_method's remaining 3.9x is DotForm handler dispatch per attribute op (37% cum pre-widening): every (. obj attr) eval pays special-form dispatch + the NewList(ItemsRef[1:]) args copy + DotForm's full feature walk. Extract DotForm's two hot shapes into callable helpers -- 2-arg attribute read and n-arg method call (incl. the __call__ StringValue marker) -- and add dotNode/dotCallNode to compileIR (obj/args via Eval so slotRefs work; attr name captured at compile). Assignment side: assignNode variant for (= (. obj attr) v) via the same helpers. Fall back to orig for descriptors-heavy paths if extraction gets risky -- compileIR's never-fails contract stays. Gate: attr_method wall-clock, conformance name-set diffs (properties, descriptors, staticmethod/classmethod, super paths must be identical).
close_reason: dotNode landed: attr_method 134->120ms (-10%), all gates green, conformance identical. Assign-side variant deliberately skipped: writes are ~2 of 8 dot ops in the profile and the bigger next lever is the discarded-error fix (filed).

title: Unbox stage 3: loop boundaries -- range induction variable, then call-ABI decision
description: (a) ForForm fast path for range iteration into a numeric-tagged slot: write the induction variable unboxed per iteration (rangeIterator currently boxes every counter -- range drives most loops in the corpus). (b) With (a) measured, make the call-boundary decision: callSlots numeric-args fast ABI (slot-to-slot without boxing, fib's n-1/n-2 shape) is only worth its complexity if the stage-2 profile still shows call-boundary convT64 high -- decide by number, file a follow-up or close with the measurement recorded.

title: Unbox stage 2: evalNum kernel (slotRef/const/operator/assign), deopt without re-eval
description: The win lands here. IR nodes gain the typed entry point: slotRef reads the scalar tag-guarded; constNode pre-classifies at compile; operatorNode arithmetic/comparisons recurse via evalNum, do raw float64 math with PromoteIntOverflow bit-identical to fastNumOp, and return unboxed; assignNode consumes unboxed results and retags -- loop-carried accumulators stop allocating entirely. CONTRACT: evalNum either fully produces (scalar, kind) or returns the boxed value it already computed; a guard miss NEVER re-evaluates a child (side effects fire exactly once) -- signature shaped like (f float64, kind uint8, boxed core.Value, err error). Box-at-escape only (generic calls, container stores, returns stay boxed this stage). Gate: micro kernel benches drop hard (target: loop-accumulate allocs/op -> 0), arith_int/iteration wall-clock, shadow-verify pass over conformance, failure-name-set diffs identical.
notes: Stage-0 alloc breakdown for KernelLoopAccum (n=1000): 2640 allocs/op post-box-cache, ~2.6/iter = counter boxes >256 (~0.74), i*2 results >256 (~0.87), accumulator boxes (~1.0). evalNum eliminates all three. MixedIntFloat at 6784 allocs (float results uncached -- FloatValue boxing is in scope here); CompareBranch 17530 (bool->stage 4 + c+1/c+2 boxes).

title: Unbox stage 1: tagged slot frame + tag choke points + shadow-verify mode
description: Land the representation with NO execution-strategy change: frames store the stage-0 winner; all reads still rebox at the slotRef boundary, all writes classify+tag at ONE choke-point setter (assignNode, callSlots binding, for targets, UnpackPattern, comprehension mini-frames -- enumerate and route ALL of them). Ship M28_DEBUG_SLOTS shadow-verification (boxed shadow slice, compare on every read, panic on divergence) and run it once across make test + test.sh + the conformance suite. Gate: zero behavior change, perf flat-to-noise (this stage may cost slightly; its job is landing the invariants, not the win).
notes: Stage-0 DECISION: parallel arrays layout (nums []float64 / tags []uint8 / refs []core.Value). Spike numbers in micro_bench_test.go Frame* benches: 3.17ns parallel vs 3.39ns struct vs 50.7ns boxed per guarded read-add-write.
close_reason: Landed: SlotFrame (parallel arrays), single Set choke point across all six binders, M28_DEBUG_SLOTS shadow mode. Zero divergences across corpus+suite+10 conformance files shadow-verified. Cost: fib kernel +4.8% (frame construction, 3 allocs vs 1) -- stage 3 target; everything else flat. Stage-2 note: relax refs-always-coherent to lazy when evalNum lands; box on demand in Get for Int/Float tags.

title: Unbox stage 0: kernel micro-benches, representation spike, small-int box cache
description: Measurement before structure. (a) Add micro-benchmarks for the numeric kernel shapes: loop-carried accumulator, mixed int/float, overflow promotion, comparison-fed branch, call boundary (fib shape) -- these are the per-stage gates. (b) Spike the two frame representations (parallel nums[]float64+tags[]uint8+vals[]core.Value vs single []struct{kind,num,ref} 24B slot) in benchmarks/micro and pick by number. (c) Cheap orthogonal win to land immediately: a package-level [256]core.Value cache for small non-negative NumberValue boxes at the box sites (mirrors Go's staticuint64s, which does NOT cover float64 -- runtime.convT64 shows in profiles). Safe for is-identity: NumberValue is a value type, interface equality is by value. (d) Re-profile arith_int/iteration/recursion_fib after the dispatch tasks land to size the boxing slice fresh.
close_reason: Stage 0 complete (2a9d4cc). DECISION: parallel arrays (nums/tags/refs) for stage 1 -- 3.17ns vs 3.39ns struct vs 50.7ns boxed. Box cache landed at CPython-parity [-5,256] (1024 measured, rejected: +0.5%). Kernels are the campaign gates; loop-accum 534us/2640 allocs -- remaining allocs are exactly the >256 boxes for stage 2. Bonus finds: s-expr in-form for loops never IR-compiled (fixed); live range iterator was protocols.RangeIterator (fixed).

title: Value unboxing: numeric slot frames + typed IR kernel (resolution layer 4)
description:
Run numeric hot paths on raw Go scalars end-to-end within a function, boxing only at escape points. This is the 'feed Go directly' move: the interpreter currently traffics in core.Value interfaces everywhere, so every int is a heap box, escape analysis is blind, and mallocgc is ~30% of every profile. Go rewards concrete types; give it concrete types.

EVIDENCE (vmspike, commit b276449): resolved tree-walk + inline ops = 10.7x vs naive; bytecode VM same boxed values = 9.5x (dispatch is irrelevant); bytecode + raw float64 = 94x. The ~10x gap between boxed and unboxed is pure value model and is available to the tree-walk. Profiles today (post 83bb43b, geomean 2.6x CPython): runtime.convT64 visible (every float64->interface boxing allocates; Go only caches small ints for integer kinds, not float64), mallocgc ~30% cum on arith/attr/comprehension cases.

DESIGN SKETCH:
- Tagged slot frames: slot i carries kind tag (INT/FLOAT/BOOL/BOXED) + unboxed scalar + boxed ref. Representation (parallel arrays vs 24B tagged struct) decided by stage-0 spike, not by taste. NaN-boxing is OUT: Go GC must see pointers in pointer-typed words.
- Typed evaluation kernel: IR nodes gain evalNum alongside evalIR. Contract: evalNum either fully produces (scalar, kind) or returns the boxed value it already computed -- NEVER re-evaluates a child on guard miss (side effects fire exactly once). slotRef reads the scalar tag-guarded; operatorNode does raw float64 math with PromoteIntOverflow semantics bit-identical to fastNumOp; assignNode stores unboxed + retags.
- Kind semantics: INT tag == core.NumberValue (float64-backed int w/ bigint promotion), FLOAT == core.FloatValue (type(1.0)==float, 4/2==2.0 -- see int-float split, 2026-07-01). type()/repr()/hash observable behavior must be byte-identical; containers and generic calls only ever see boxed values (box at escape).
- Always-correct fallback at every level: guard miss -> generic path; ineligible function -> boxed slots (today's engine); tag invariant violations caught by stage-1 shadow-verify mode.

INVARIANT THAT KILLS US IF MISSED: every write path to a slot must maintain the tag -- assignNode, callSlots arg binding, for-loop targets, UnpackPattern, comprehension mini-frames. Mitigation: single choke-point setter + M28_DEBUG_SLOTS shadow-verification mode (store boxed shadow, compare on read, panic on divergence) run once over the full conformance suite per stage.

RISKS: numeric tower interop (int/float/bigint/bool -- see numbers-tower notes), is-identity (NumberValue is a value type, interface equality is by value, small-int box cache is safe), guard misprediction on type-unstable locals (deopt must be cheap), double-eval bugs in deopt (contract above).

EXPECTATION SETTING: vmspike's 10x is pure-numeric; macro cases are mixed. Realistic: arith_int/iteration/recursion_fib large wins, geomean 2.6x -> ~1.8-2.0x, with the pure-numeric cases at or past CPython parity. Judge by M28 absolute ms per LOOP.md; every stage gates on make test + test.sh + conformance failure-name-set diffs (the gate that caught all five traps in the 83bb43b widening).

ORDERING: independent of, but sequenced after, the two dispatch tasks (dotNode IR, try/except modeling) -- re-profile after those land so stage 0 sizes the boxing slice against fresh numbers. Parent perf epic: M28-e42e. IR spec bead M28-a88f must absorb the tag/kind contract when stage 2 lands.

All gates green; 16 conformance files identical/improved. Push pending SSH agent.

title: perf: comprehensions bypass resolution layer; one comp de-optimizes whole enclosing function
description: comprehension case 8.6x CPython. (a) resolve.go:97 bails whole function on any comp form; (b) comp bodies eval fully generic per element (map lookup per name, GetOperator per op, arg-slice allocs). Plan: hygiene in comprehensionLoop, mini-IR compile of comp body (var in slot) cached per form, resolver containment via free-symbol rule.

title: perf: O(n) Items() copy on per-element list access made indexed loops quadratic
description: listMethodGetItem (__getitem__), list get method, and protocols.ListIterator.Next copied the whole backing slice via Items() to read one element -> indexed loops and protocol iteration were O(n^2). Fixed by direct backing-slice reads (ItemsRef / package-private items); __getitem__ slice path also switched to ItemsRef. list_ops 274->48ms (-83%), 8.8x->1.6x CPython; N=50k indexed loop 31.8s->0.38s; geomean 3.7x->3.3x. Fixed in commit 134ca36.
close_reason: Fixed in 134ca36: direct backing-slice reads at all three sites; all gates green (make test, test.sh, micro allocs identical); baseline re-locked

title: Widen slot/IR eligibility until the IR path is the common path
description: eval/resolve.go gates slot compilation to simple positional signatures and bails on closures, defaults/kwargs/starargs, try/with, comprehensions, tuple targets; resolveBody bails on keyword/unpack calls. Extend coverage construct by construct, keeping the always-correct fallback, until raw s-expr walking is only the REPL/macro/bootstrap path. This is perf ladder rung 2 (vmspike resolved-dispatch 2.3x) and the architectural centerpiece of M28-b5c6.

title: Closure cells: real cell objects and __closure__
description: Closures capture the defining Context via the parent chain; __closure__ returns a dummy (eval/function_calls.go) and analyzeLocals rejects any closure-touching function from slot compilation (eval/resolve.go). Introduce real cells (shared slots) so captured locals work with slot frames. Prerequisite for widening IR eligibility.

title: Resolver as single arbiter: head symbols resolve through scope
description: Meaning is currently decided before scope: special forms dispatch from a map ahead of user bindings (eval/evaluator.go; the quote-yields-to-binding hack patches one case) and Context.Lookup consults the operator registry before any scope (core/context.go), so operators and form names are not shadowable; module contexts also check ModuleDict before local Vars. Move the decision to resolve/lower time: a head symbol is a special form only if scope resolution does not bind it. Fixes the shadowing bug class (async sleep/select/go collisions still latent) and deletes per-eval map lookups.

title: Unify module caches and loaders
description: Three module caches are reconciled by hand: core.ModuleRegistry (core/module_registry.go), modules.moduleCache (modules/registry.go), and sys.modules, with two registerInSysModules implementations (core/module_loader_enhanced.go and modules/python_loader.go). Single cache with sys.modules as the one source of truth; delete the dead DefaultModuleLoader (core/module_loader.go).

title: Unify method dispatch registries (TypeDescriptor vs MethodRegistry)
description: Two mechanisms exist: typeRegistry/TypeDescriptor (core/type_descriptor.go, populated by core/type_registry_*.go) and MethodRegistry (core/method_registry.go, used by Generator and others), with 84 distinct GetAttr implementations on top. Standardize on TypeDescriptor, migrate stragglers, delete the no-op documentation shims in builtin/methods/.

title: Key dicts and sets by hash/eq instead of ValueToKey string encoding
description: DictValue/SetValue (core/containers.go) key a Go map by a string encoding from core.ValueToKey (core/utilities.go) plus parallel keys/orderedKeys bookkeeping. Custom __hash__/__eq__ semantics, numeric-key edge cases, and per-op fmt.Sprintf allocations all fall out of this. Move to hash/eq-based storage honoring Python hashing (hash(1) == hash(1.0) already special-cased). Related: M28-jmst.73 (test_hash).

title: Replace BFS method resolution with real C3 linearization
description: core/class.go GetMethod/GetMethodWithClass and the __mro__ construction do breadth-first traversal with dedup while comments claim C3; diamond inheritance diverges from CPython. Implement C3 once and use it for both method lookup and __mro__. Also revisit the dict/str subclass BackingDict/BackingStr special-casing that bypasses the MRO.

title: Single format-spec engine for format(), f-strings, and str.format
description: Three parallel implementations: builtin/string_format.go (parseFormatSpecString/applyFormatSpec), core/string_methods.go formatValueWithSpec (used by str.format - known divergence: grouping separators like {:,} are dropped), and common/types/dunder.go CallFormat; f-string spec parsing also exists twice (parser/fstring.go and parser/python_tokenizer.go). Route everything through one engine. Root-cause fix toward M28-jmst.57 (test_format).

title: Consolidate arithmetic dispatch to one canonical path
description: Addition alone has at least five implementations: builtin/operators/operators_arithmetic.go (addTwo + types.Switch fallback), core/type_registry_primitives.go dunders, builtin/types.go int/float Class methods storing __value__, core/protocols/numeric.go NumericOps, and eval/resolve_ir.go fastNumOp; bigint promotion is duplicated between builtin/operators/bigint_arith.go and core/primitives.go PromoteIntOverflow. Collapse to one canonical dispatch in the runtime layer; fast paths remain only as provably-equivalent inlinings of it, gated by the conformance suite. Unblocks trusting operatorNode in the IR.

title: Runtime consolidation: one implementation per semantic
description:
One implementation per semantic.

M28's Python semantics are implemented several times in parallel, and every duplication is a conformance-divergence factory: addition exists in at least five places, format-spec parsing in three, method registries twice, module caches three times, the MRO is BFS while comments claim C3, and dicts are keyed by a string encoding of the key. The resolver/IR work (M28-b5c6) cannot trust a canonical path until there is one.

Rule for all children: collapse to ONE canonical implementation in the runtime layer; fast paths may remain only as provably-equivalent inlinings of it, with the conformance suite (M28-a6b8) as the gate. Debt register with file anchors: docs/design/compiler-architecture.md.

title: Module: Implement _thread C extension
description:
Implement the _thread C extension module in Go.

This module provides low-level threading primitives that threading.py depends on.

Required exports:
- start_new_thread(function, args[, kwargs])
- allocate_lock() -> lock object
- get_ident() -> int
- _local class for thread-local storage
- LockType
- _ExceptHookArgs
- TIMEOUT_MAX
- error exception
- interrupt_main()
- exit()
- stack_size([size])

Many CPython tests require threading support.

Reference: https://docs.python.org/3/library/_thread.html
close_reason: Completed _thread module with all required exports: start_new_thread, allocate_lock, get_ident, _local, LockType, _ExceptHookArgs, TIMEOUT_MAX, error, interrupt_main, exit, stack_size, etc.

title: Parser: Support pattern matching (match/case)
description:
Implement PEP 634 pattern matching syntax.

Required for: test_patma.py and many other tests

Syntax to support:
```python
match subject:
    case pattern1:
        action1
    case pattern2 if guard:
        action2
    case _:
        default_action
```

Patterns to support:
- Literal patterns (1, 'hello', True, None)
- Capture patterns (name)
- Wildcard pattern (_)
- Class patterns (Point(x, y))
- Sequence patterns ([a, b, *rest])
- Mapping patterns ({'key': value})
- OR patterns (pattern1 | pattern2)
- AS patterns (pattern as name)
- Guards (case x if x > 0)

Reference: PEP 634, 635, 636
close_reason: Implemented full pattern matching: literals, wildcards, capture, class, OR, AS, star (*rest), and mapping patterns

title: Pass CPython 3.12 regression tests
description:
Run CPython's standard library regression tests from ~/.pyenv/versions/3.12.0/lib/python3.12/test/ to prove Python 3 compatibility.

## Current Status (2025-12-31)

**Passing tests:**
- test_bool.py - 31 tests OK
- test_augassign.py - 7 tests OK  
- test_keyword.py - 11 tests OK

**Recently added C extensions to unblock more tests:**
- math.lgamma, math.gamma, math.fabs
- itertools.accumulate
- _random.Random (with random, seed, getrandbits, getstate, setstate)
- _bisect (bisect_left, bisect_right, insort_left, insort_right)
- _sha512.sha512

**Common remaining blockers:**
- Missing `gc` module (test_dict, test_tuple, test_enumerate)
- Missing `time.process_time` (test_int)
- Missing `fractions`/`decimal` modules (regex `(?=` not supported)
- Missing `subprocess`/`fcntl` modules (test_stat, test_graphlib)
- Parse errors on some test files (special chars in test data)
- `reversed()` via `__getitem__` not implemented (test_sort)

Focus on core language features, pure Python stdlib modules, and avoid OS-specific, threading, networking, GUI, and CPython-internal tests.
notes:
## Session Update (2025-12-31)

Added new C extensions:
- gc module (stub) - enables gc.collect(), gc.isenabled(), etc.
- _lzma._encode_filter_properties, _decode_filter_properties - fixes lzma import
- math.modf - needed by _pydatetime

**Currently passing tests:**

title: Compiler Architecture Phase 2: Optimize HIR
description: Implement constant folding pass, dead code elimination, inline simple functions, add optimization flags to CLI. Deliverable: Parser → HIR → Optimize → Adapter → core.Value → Evaluator
design: Add optimization passes on HIR. Timeline: 1 month. See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md Phase 2
close_reason: Speculative IR optimization passes (constant folding/DCE/inlining) are off the plan; optimization is measurement-driven via benchmarks/LOOP.md and M28-e42e. Folding can land inside eval/resolve_ir.go compileIR if a profile ever justifies it.

title: Frontend interface + dialect selection (kill the double-parse fallback)
description: executeM28File tries the full Python parser and falls back to the s-expr parser (main.go), and EvalString does the same (eval/eval_string.go): double parse cost and error messages that blame the wrong grammar. Define a Frontend interface (Parse -> surface forms + locations), select by file extension or pragma. Package moves to frontend/ are deferred until the consolidation epic M28-j9o lands - moving files does not move semantics.
design: Clean frontend/backend separation. Timeline: 1-2 months. See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md Phase 3

title: IR spec: document and version the resolved IR
description: The IR exists (slotRef plus the irNode family in eval/resolve.go and eval/resolve_ir.go); what is missing is the contract: node inventory, scoping and slot rules, eligibility gates, fallback semantics, and how frontends target surface forms. Write docs/design/ir-spec.md and version it. NOT the v1 plan (a new hir/ package with adapters): the s-expr surface layer stays, because macros, the REPL, and metaprogramming live there.
design: Split IR from runtime values. Timeline: 1-2 months. See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md Phase 1

title: Compiler Architecture Phase 4: Bytecode Backend
description: Design instruction set, implement HIR → Bytecode compiler, implement bytecode VM, add optimization (type specialization), add .m28c serialization. Deliverable: 3-10x speedup, .m28c file format, CLI commands
design: Add bytecode VM for performance. Timeline: 2-3 months. See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md Phase 4
close_reason: Superseded by the vmspike experiment (commit b276449): at the same value model a resolved tree-walk equals a bytecode VM; bytecode buys nothing for speed. Speed work continues on the resolution ladder (M28-e42e, M28-b5c6); a frame tier for resumable semantics only is M28-8jo. See docs/design/mir-bytecode-vm.md.

title: MIR Phase 4: Optimization

title: Compiler Architecture Migration
description:
Multi-frontend, single-runtime platform. Frontends (Python, M28) lower to shared surface forms; the resolver plus typed IR (eval/resolve.go, eval/resolve_ir.go) is the compiler; execution is a resolved tree-walk.

The vmspike experiment (commit b276449) settled the execution question: at the same value model a bytecode VM adds nothing over a resolved tree-walk, so there are no bytecode/LLVM/WASM backend tiers - those children are closed with reasons.

Current children: IR spec (M28-a88f), resolver-as-arbiter (M28-ehi), closure cells (M28-osj), eligibility widening (M28-6mf), frontend interface + dialect selection (M28-a472), frame tier for resumable semantics, deferred (M28-8jo). Runtime consolidation (M28-j9o) is a de facto prerequisite: the resolver can only trust a canonical semantic path once there is one.

See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md (v2, 2026-07).

title: AST Layer for Multiple Frontend Support

title: perf: exception raise/catch path costs ~21us per exception
description: Function-level try loop with raise-per-iteration runs at ~21us/exception (4.3s for 200k) -- dominates any exception-heavy code and the exceptions macro case (5.7x CPython). Profile the raise->errorToExceptionInstance->match->handler chain; suspects from reading: fresh exception Instance construction per raise, __context__ chain walk, traceback creation, per-raise map writes (__current_exception__ save/restore), string formatting in error paths. Related: tryNode bead (clause re-parse).

title: perf: tryNode -- pre-parse try clause structure at compile time
description: tryForm re-parses its full clause structure (partition, except-shape classification, hasAsAfter walks) on EVERY execution -- the dotNode treatment applies: compileIR lowers modeled try forms to a tryNode holding pre-classified clauses (body stmts, typed/bare handlers with compiled bodies, else/finally), evalIR runs the same match/finally logic against them. Quiet-try microbench is 1574ms/300k iter (~5us) post-containment; clause re-parse is a chunk of that. Prereq familiarity: resolveTryForm shapes (7d1ed7f). Bigger sibling target: the raise path itself costs ~21us/exception (errorToExceptionInstance + matching + context chaining) -- profile before choosing.

title: perf: stop constructing discarded errors on attribute miss (retry at new baseline)
description: TypeDescriptor.GetAttribute builds fmt.Errorf("'%s' object has no attribute...") on every miss and ~16 callers discard it (e.g. NumberValue.GetAttr under DotForm). Previously tried and REVERTED as wall-clock-flat when attr_method was 4946ms; at 120ms post-dotNode it profiles at ~5% (fmt.doPrintf under DotForm/operatorNode). Redo: sentinel or ok-bool miss path (GetAttributeOK), update discarding callers, keep the formatted error only where it propagates to users. Verify wall-clock this time, not just allocs (the old lesson) -- and conformance name-set diffs since attr-miss messages are user-visible where they DO propagate.

title: Unbox stage 5: IR-spec tag/kind contract, re-baseline, LOOP.md targets
description: Absorb the tag/kind contract into the IR spec (M28-a88f): kind lattice, tag invariants + choke points, evalNum deopt contract, box-at-escape rule, shadow-verify mode. make bench-update-baseline to lock the campaign result; rewrite benchmarks/LOOP.md 'current targets' from the new profile; update the architecture doc state line.

title: Unbox stage 4: bool kind + ifNode evalBool; escape-site box cache if stage 0 said yes
description: Comparisons feed branches without boxing: BOOL kind in the tag set, ifNode/whileForm condition path via evalBool, comparison operators produce unboxed bools inside the kernel. Fold in the escape-site small-value box cache if the stage-0 spike measured a win at box sites that remain hot. Gate: comparison-fed-branch micro bench, no conformance drift (bool/int equality: True==1, hash(True)==hash(1) already covered by kind semantics).

title: Extend native numeric fast paths to /, //, %, **
description: Operator specialization (perf ladder rung 1) covers +, -, *, <, <=, >, >=, ==, != (builtin/operators/operators_arithmetic.go family and eval/resolve_ir.go operatorNode). Remaining: /, //, %, ** with int/float/bigint promotion semantics preserved (4/2 == 2.0, overflow promotion).

title: Native re fast path: bypass re.py wrapper via c_sre
description: import re resolves to CPython re/__init__.py, so every match runs its pure-Python wrapper on the interpreter (~2.1ms per module-level re.search vs ~0.57ms for a native pattern object; re._cache works - this is interpreter overhead, not cache misses). Route module-level match/search/escape and Pattern methods to modules/c_sre.go natively while preserving semantics. Unblocks regex_match (worst macro bench case), test_strftime/test_calendar timeouts, and M28-jmst.80.

title: Bundle a pinned Python stdlib; remove the python3 shell-out
description: modules/python_finder.go discoverPythonPaths shells out to python3 to discover sys.path, so the single-static-binary story silently depends on a CPython install. Pin and ship a stdlib snapshot (go:embed or a versioned install dir on M28_PATH), make discovery work without python3, keep env overrides for development.

title: Delete dead code: env/, LispNumber/LispString, legacy parser family, string_legacy, dual param binding
description: Unreferenced or vestigial: env/ package (no importers); core/lisp_number.go and core/lisp_string.go; the old char-based recursive-descent family inside parser/parser.go (parseAtom/parseList/parseString/parseFString/parsePostfix, only self-referenced); builtin/string_legacy.go; UserFunction dual param representations (legacy params vs signature, eval/function_calls.go) with parallel binding paths; debug scaffolding in hot paths (evaluator.go logThis block, commented PurePath tracing in function_calls.go).

title: Beyond-CPython product layer
description: Where "more useful than CPython alone" lives. M28 will not beat CPython on its own turf; the differentiation is around the engine: an embeddable Go API (M28-6cc6) - "Lua for Go, but Python"; a genuinely self-contained single binary (bundled stdlib, no python3 dependency); capability-based sandboxing per interpreter; an isolate+channels concurrency story with no GIL and no shared-heap free threading; macros and dual syntax over Python semantics; wasm/wasip1 builds for browser and portable deployment. See docs/design/compiler-architecture.md (product layer).

title: Class: metaclass __new__ return value not used
description:
When a metaclass's __new__ method returns something other than a class (e.g., a tuple), M28 ignores the return value and creates a normal class anyway. Python uses the return value as the class object.

Example:
```python
class Meta(int):
    def __new__(cls, name, bases, attrs, **kwargs):
        return (bases, kwargs)  # Should become the class

class A(metaclass=Meta): pass
# In Python: A == ((), {})
# In M28: A == <class 'A'>  # Wrong - ignores __new__ return
```

Found in: test_class.py:testClassWithExtCall
close_reason: Fixed metaclass __new__ to use return value for class binding, even when non-class

title: Performance optimization epic
description:
Make M28 fast, evidence-first.

State (2026-07-01): macro geomean ~3.7x CPython (was ~126x at campaign start). The wins came from the ValueToKey/getGoroutineID fix, the GOGC=400 default, resolution layers 1-3 (eval/resolve.go, eval/resolve_ir.go - scope_lookup is near CPython parity), and native operator fast paths.

Remaining ladder, in order: finish operator specialization (/, //, %, ** - M28-2gb), re-profile from the current baseline, then decide on value unboxing (the path past CPython; the vmspike unboxed rung measured 94x). regex_match is the worst case and needs the native re path (M28-dgh), not micro-tweaks.

Method: judge changes by M28 absolute ms (the vs base column), never the noisy CPython ratio; alloc-count wins without wall-clock wins do not count; GC tuning before big rewrites. Runbook: benchmarks/LOOP.md. Philosophy unchanged: correctness first, measure before optimizing, no optimization without before/after numbers.
design:
## Approach

1. **Phase 1: Measurement Infrastructure**
   - Build benchmark suite
   - Set up profiling tools
   - Establish baseline metrics

2. **Phase 2: Low-Hanging Fruit**
   - Profile and find obvious bottlenecks
   - Fix allocation-heavy code paths
   - Optimize most-used builtins

3. **Phase 3: Architectural Improvements**
   - Consider bytecode if needed
   - Module caching system
   - Memory pooling

4. **Phase 4: Continuous Optimization**
   - Performance regression tests in CI
   - Regular profiling reviews
   - Community-reported hotspots
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Benchmark suite exists and runs in CI
- [ ] Baseline performance metrics documented
- [ ] No performance regressions in CI
- [ ] Startup time meets target (<50ms)
- [ ] Common operations within 10-20x of CPython
notes: 2026-07-02: geomean now 2.6x CPython (list-access O(1) fix 134ca36, comprehension IR d37414f, key-alloc cuts d7ee8c4, resolution-layer widening to methods 83bb43b). Value unboxing is now SCOPED: epic M28-9pm, six gated stages (representation spike -> tagged frames + shadow-verify -> evalNum kernel -> loop/call boundaries -> bool kind -> spec+re-baseline), sequenced after the two remaining dispatch tasks M28-hu8 (dotNode IR) and M28-v1r (try/except modeling).

title: Design and implement embeddable Go API for M28 engine
description:
Create a clean, idiomatic Go API that makes M28 easy to embed in other Go programs. The goal is to enable Go developers to add Python-like scripting capabilities to their applications with minimal boilerplate.

## Target Use Cases

1. **Configuration files** - Use M28/Python syntax for application config with computed values
2. **Plugin systems** - Allow users to write extensible plugins in familiar Python syntax
3. **Game scripting** - Game engines needing a scripting layer for game logic
4. **Automation tools** - CI/CD, deployment scripts, task runners
5. **Data transformation** - ETL pipelines, data processing scripts
6. **Rule engines** - Business logic, validation rules, policies
7. **Interactive REPLs** - Embed a REPL in CLI tools or IDEs
8. **Template engines** - Dynamic content generation with logic
9. **Testing/validation** - User-defined test scripts and validators

## Proposed API Design

### 1. Simple Initialization
```go
// Basic engine
engine := m28.New()

// With options (functional options pattern)
engine := m28.New(
    m28.WithStdlib(),           // Load standard library
    m28.WithModulePath("./lib"), // Add module search path
    m28.WithGlobals(globals),    // Pre-set global variables
)
```

### 2. Evaluation
```go
// Evaluate string
result, err := engine.Eval("1 + 2 * 3")

// Evaluate with variables
result, err := engine.Eval("x + y", m28.Vars{"x": 10, "y": 20})

// Evaluate file
result, err := engine.EvalFile("script.py")

// Evaluate with context (for timeouts/cancellation)
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 5*time.Second)
result, err := engine.EvalContext(ctx, "long_computation()")
```

### 3. Go Function Registration
```go
// Simple function
engine.RegisterFunc("greet", func(name string) string {
    return "Hello, " + name + "!"
})

// Variadic function
engine.RegisterFunc("sum", func(nums ...int) int {
    total := 0
    for _, n := range nums { total += n }
    return total
})

// Function with error return
engine.RegisterFunc("divide", func(a, b float64) (float64, error) {
    if b == 0 { return 0, errors.New("division by zero") }
    return a / b, nil
})

// Register struct methods
engine.RegisterType("MyService", &MyService{})
```

### 4. Variable Access
```go
// Set Go values as M28 globals
engine.Set("config", map[string]interface{}{
    "debug": true,
    "port": 8080,
})
engine.Set("users", []string{"alice", "bob"})

// Get M28 values as Go types
port := engine.GetInt("config.port")
debug := engine.GetBool("config.debug")
result := engine.Get("computed_value")
```

### 5. Type Conversion Utilities
```go
// Convert M28 values to Go types
intVal, err := m28.AsInt(result)
strVal, err := m28.AsString(result)
listVal, err := m28.AsSlice[int](result)
mapVal, err := m28.AsMap[string, any](result)

// Convert Go values to M28 values
m28Val := m28.FromGo(myGoStruct)
```

### 6. Sandboxing and Security
```go
engine := m28.New(m28.WithSandbox(m28.SandboxConfig{
    AllowFileRead:  true,   // Allow reading files
    AllowFileWrite: false,  // Disallow writing files
    AllowNetwork:   false,  // No network access
    AllowExec:      false,  // No subprocess execution
    MaxMemory:      100<<20, // 100MB memory limit
    MaxCPUTime:     5*time.Second,
    AllowedModules: []string{"math", "json", "re"},
}))
```

### 7. Module/Plugin System
```go
// Create custom module
mod := m28.NewModule("myapp")
mod.Set("version", "1.0.0")
mod.RegisterFunc("do_thing", doThing)
engine.RegisterModule("myapp", mod)

// In M28: import myapp; myapp.do_thing()
```

### 8. Error Handling
```go
result, err := engine.Eval(script)
if err != nil {
    var syntaxErr *m28.SyntaxError
    var runtimeErr *m28.RuntimeError
    
    if errors.As(err, &syntaxErr) {
        log.Printf("Syntax error at %s:%d: %s", 
            syntaxErr.File, syntaxErr.Line, syntaxErr.Message)
    } else if errors.As(err, &runtimeErr) {
        log.Printf("Runtime error: %s\nTraceback:\n%s",
            runtimeErr.Message, runtimeErr.Traceback)
    }
}
```

### 9. REPL Support
```go
repl := m28.NewREPL(engine)
repl.SetPrompt(">>> ")
repl.SetContinuationPrompt("... ")
repl.Run(os.Stdin, os.Stdout)

// Or integrate with custom I/O
repl.Eval(line) // Returns result, needsMore, error
```

## Implementation Tasks

1. [ ] Design and document public API surface
2. [ ] Create `m28` package with clean exports (hide internal details)
3. [ ] Implement functional options pattern for engine configuration
4. [ ] Add automatic Go<->M28 type conversion using reflection
5. [ ] Implement Go function registration with reflection
6. [ ] Add struct/method registration support
7. [ ] Implement sandboxing with resource limits
8. [ ] Add context.Context support for cancellation
9. [ ] Create proper error types with location info
10. [ ] Add module creation API for Go-defined modules
11. [ ] Implement embeddable REPL
12. [ ] Write comprehensive documentation and examples
13. [ ] Add benchmarks for embedding overhead
14. [ ] Create example applications demonstrating each use case
design:
## Architecture

```
m28/                          # Public embedding package
├── engine.go                 # Engine type and core API
├── options.go                # Functional options
├── convert.go                # Go<->M28 type conversion
├── register.go               # Function/type registration
├── sandbox.go                # Sandboxing implementation
├── module.go                 # Custom module creation
├── repl.go                   # Embeddable REPL
├── errors.go                 # Public error types
└── examples/
    ├── config/               # Config file example
    ├── plugin/               # Plugin system example
    ├── game/                 # Game scripting example
    └── repl/                 # Custom REPL example
```

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Functional Options** - Use `m28.New(opts...)` pattern for extensibility
2. **Zero Dependencies** - Public API should not expose internal types
3. **Reflection-based Registration** - Auto-convert Go functions without manual wrapping
4. **Generics for Type Safety** - Use Go 1.18+ generics where beneficial
5. **Context Support** - All long-running operations should accept context
6. **Sensible Defaults** - Work out of the box, customize when needed
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Go developers can embed M28 with <10 lines of code
- [ ] Documentation includes working examples for each use case
- [ ] Type conversion handles common Go types (int, string, slice, map, struct)
- [ ] Go functions can be called from M28 without manual wrapping
- [ ] Sandboxing can restrict file, network, and exec access
- [ ] context.Context cancellation stops script execution
- [ ] Error messages include file, line, and traceback info
- [ ] No internal types leak through the public API
- [ ] Benchmarks show <1ms overhead for simple evaluations

title: Audit codebase for fmt.Errorf usage that should be structured errors
description:
Many places in the codebase return generic `fmt.Errorf()` errors instead of structured error types like `*core.TypeError`, `*core.ValueError`, etc. This causes:

1. Loss of exception type information when errors cross Go/Python boundary
2. Python code cannot catch specific exception types (e.g., `except TypeError`)
3. Harder to debug because all errors become generic "Exception"

Recent example: `len()` was returning `fmt.Errorf` instead of `*core.TypeError`, causing TypeError to appear as generic Exception in Python code.

This issue tracks a comprehensive audit to:
- Find all `fmt.Errorf` calls that should be structured errors
- Replace with appropriate typed errors from core package
- Ensure Python exception types are preserved correctly

Common places to check:
- builtin/ package functions
- Type validation and conversion functions
- Attribute access and method calls
- Container operations (index, slice, etc.)
- Arithmetic operations
- String/bytes operations

Available structured error types:
- `*core.TypeError` - type mismatches, invalid operations
- `*core.ValueError` - invalid values
- `*core.KeyError` - missing dict/map keys
- `*core.IndexError` - invalid index access
- `*core.AttributeError` - missing attributes
- `*core.NameError` - undefined names
- `*core.ZeroDivisionError` - division by zero
- `*core.ImportError` / `*core.ModuleNotFoundError` - import failures
- `*core.OSError` / `*core.FileNotFoundError` - file/OS errors
- `*core.AssertionError` - assertion failures
- `*core.StopIteration` - iterator exhausted
design:
Approach:
1. Grep for `fmt.Errorf` across codebase
2. For each occurrence, determine if it should be a structured error
3. Check error message to identify appropriate error type:
   - "expected X, got Y" → TypeError
   - "invalid value" / "out of range" → ValueError  
   - "key not found" / "no such key" → KeyError
   - "index out of range" → IndexError
   - "has no attribute" / "no such attribute" → AttributeError
   - "not defined" / "undefined" → NameError
   - "division by zero" → ZeroDivisionError
4. Replace with appropriate structured error
5. Add tests to verify exception type preservation
6. Can be done incrementally by package

Packages to audit (priority order):
1. builtin/ - most user-facing, highest impact
2. core/ - core operations
3. eval/ - expression evaluation
4. modules/ - module operations
5. parser/ - less critical (parse errors have own types)
acceptance_criteria:
- Audit completed for all packages
- All user-facing errors use appropriate structured types
- Python code can catch specific exception types correctly
- Tests verify exception type preservation
- Documentation of when to use which error type
- Style guide updated with error handling guidelines

title: range() with bigint bounds collapses to empty range
description: range(2**60, 2**60+10) -> both bounds become 9.223372036854776e+18 (RangeValue stores float64; bigint args mangled at construction), so the range is empty and sum/list/iteration silently return nothing. CPython handles arbitrary int bounds. Pre-existing (verified on pre-2026-07-02 binary); found while gating the sum closed form, whose isExactInt guard correctly declines these. Fix needs RangeValue to carry exact ints (int64 + bigint fallback) or reject construction. Low priority: 2^53+ range bounds are rare.

title: Frame-based execution tier for resumable semantics (deferred)
description: The one honest reason for a bytecode/frame representation is resumable frames: generators run on a bespoke step machine (eval/generator_exec.go), async wraps goroutines, and recursion depth is bounded by Go stacks. When that pressure is real, build a frame-based interpreter over the resolved IR - same value model, same runtime layer. Explicitly NOT a speed tier (vmspike, commit b276449). Design constraints: docs/design/mir-bytecode-vm.md.

title: Define the isolate-per-interpreter concurrency model

The credible no-GIL story is isolates: one interpreter per domain, message passing over channels, no shared mutable Python heap. Document the model, audit global state (operator registry, type registry, module caches) for isolate safety, expose it in the embed API. Explicit non-goal: shared-heap free threading.

title: Support complex variable unpacking in comprehensions
description:
Python supports tuple/list unpacking in comprehension for clauses:

```python
[(x*y) for (x, y) in [(1,2), (3,4), (5,6)]]
# → [2, 12, 30]

[a+b+c for (a, (b, c)) in [(1, (2, 3)), (4, (5, 6))]]
# → [6, 15]
```

This requires parser and evaluator changes to support pattern matching in the loop variable position.
design:
1. Update parser to allow complex patterns in for clause variable:
   - parseLoopVariables() should handle tuple/list patterns
   - Build AST node representing the pattern

2. Update evaluator to unpack values:
   - When binding loop variable, check if it's a pattern
   - Unpack iterable value according to pattern
   - Bind each sub-variable in the pattern

3. Handle errors gracefully:
   - ValueError if unpacking size mismatch
   - Clear error messages

4. Test cases:
   - Simple tuple unpacking
   - Nested tuple unpacking  
   - List unpacking
   - Mixed with multiple for clauses
acceptance_criteria:
- Simple tuple unpacking works: `[(x,y) for (x,y) in pairs]`
- Nested unpacking works: `[a+b+c for (a,(b,c)) in data]`
- Unpacking in nested comprehensions works
- Proper error messages for unpacking failures
- Tests added covering all patterns
close_reason: Verified tuple and nested unpacking work in comprehensions

title: Support walrus operator in comprehensions
description:
Python 3.8+ supports walrus operator (:=) in comprehensions for intermediate value assignment:

```python
[y for x in range(10) if (y := x*2) > 10]
# y is assigned in the condition, used in expression
# → [12, 14, 16, 18]

[(y, x) for x in range(5) if (y := expensive_func(x)) > threshold]
# Avoid calling expensive_func twice
```

This requires:
1. Walrus operator implementation (if not already done)
2. Proper scoping (walrus variables shouldn't leak)
3. Variable visibility across clauses
design:
1. Verify walrus operator is implemented in parser
2. Add scoping rules for comprehensions:
   - Walrus assignments visible within comprehension scope
   - Don't leak to outer scope
3. Update evaluator to maintain comprehension scope
4. Test cases:
   - Walrus in condition
   - Walrus in expression
   - Walrus in nested comprehensions
   - Scope isolation tests
acceptance_criteria:
- Walrus operator works in comprehension conditions
- Walrus operator works in comprehension expressions
- Variables don't leak outside comprehension
- Walrus variables visible to subsequent clauses
- Tests cover all usage patterns
close_reason: Verified walrus operator works in list and dict comprehensions

title: Implement zlib C extension module
description:
Implement zlib compression module using Go's compress/zlib and compress/flate packages.

Core API to implement:
- compress(data, level=-1) - Compress bytes
- decompress(data, wbits=15, bufsize=16384) - Decompress bytes
- compressobj(level=-1, method=DEFLATED, wbits=15, memLevel=8, strategy=0) - Compression object
- decompressobj(wbits=15) - Decompression object
- adler32(data, value=1) - Compute Adler-32 checksum
- crc32(data, value=0) - Compute CRC-32 checksum

Constants:
- DEFLATED, Z_DEFAULT_COMPRESSION, Z_BEST_SPEED, Z_BEST_COMPRESSION, etc.

Implementation strategy:
- Create modules/c_zlib.go
- Use compress/zlib for basic operations
- Use compress/flate for compression objects
- Use hash/crc32 and custom Adler-32 implementation

Estimated complexity: Medium (Go has good support, need to wrap API)
acceptance_criteria:
- compress() and decompress() work with bytes
- Compression objects support incremental compression
- Checksums match Python/zlib values
- Can compress/decompress data round-trip
- Compatible with Python's zlib module

title: Implement _bz2 C extension module
description:
Implement bzip2 compression module using Go's compress/bzip2 package.

Core API to implement:
- BZ2Compressor class - Compression object
  - compress(data) - Compress data
  - flush() - Finish compression
- BZ2Decompressor class - Decompression object
  - decompress(data, max_length=-1) - Decompress data
  - eof property - Check if end of stream

Implementation strategy:
- Create modules/c_bz2.go
- Use compress/bzip2 for decompression
- Use external bzip2 library for compression (Go stdlib only has reader)
- Implement classes with state

Estimated complexity: Medium-High (Go bzip2 support is read-only, may need CGo or external lib)
acceptance_criteria:
- BZ2Compressor can compress data
- BZ2Decompressor can decompress data
- Round-trip compression works
- Compatible with Python's bz2 module
- No CGo dependencies (prefer pure Go)
